resources.string:
When TalkBack is on, it provides spoken feedback so that you can use your device without looking at the screen. This can be helpful for people who are blind or have low vision.

To navigate using TalkBack:
• Swipe right or left to move between items
• Double-tap to activate an item
• Drag two fingers to scroll

To turn off TalkBack:
• Tap the switch. You’ll see an outline. Double-tap the switch.
• On the confirmation message, tap OK. Then double-tap OK.

When TalkBack is on, it provides spoken feedback so that you can use your device without looking at the screen. TalkBack is intended for situations or people who have difficulty seeing the screen.

How to use TalkBack
• Swipe right or left to move between items
• Double-tap to activate an item
• Drag 2 fingers to scroll
How to turn off TalkBack
1. In Settings, tap Use TalkBack. There will be an outline. Double-tap it.
2. On the confirmation message, tap Stop. There will be an outline. Double-tap it.
Use a screen reader to hear items spoken aloud

There are currently no custom labels

How to create a custom label
Navigate to an unlabelled image and add a label from the TalkBack menu
Speed at which the text is spoken
Speak notifications when screen is off
Always speak number of list items
Speak count for all scrolling methods
Cover proximity sensor to stop speech
When you select keyboard keys or navigate by character, you\'ll hear an example along with each letter, like \'h, hotel\'
Announces the count of repeated symbols when there are 4 or more
After describing content, suggest how to use
When you turn on the screen
Includes info while entering and exiting lists, grids and other containers
Focus delay added to reading control
Swipe with 3 fingers to use reading control and change the focus delay at any time

When typing focus delay is set below 0.3 seconds, double-tap to type will be very difficult to perform. Choose 'Switch' to automatically switch to lift to type.

To use lift to type, hold your finger to select any key, then lift for typing.

You can customise this in TalkBack settings.
Typing focus delay added to reading control
If you have issues with double-tap while typing, swipe with 3 fingers to use reading control and change to a longer typing focus delay
Typing focus delay is not functioning without lift to type.
This will reset all delay settings to default
The latency reduction settings have been reset.
This will reset all gestures to their default actions.\n\nYou will lose any changes that you have made.
The gesture settings have been reset.
Includes updates while downloading, following driving directions and more. When turned off, you\'ll still receive less frequent updates.
Speak system and input windows, such as when you switch to the status bar, notifications or another keyboard
These settings can reduce the delay before TalkBack announcements
Tap once on a focused item to activate. Faster navigation than double-tapping.
To use the TalkBack menu, tap the screen with three fingers. You can also swipe up then right, or swipe down then right.
To use the TalkBack menu, turn your secondary palm in and pinch.
To use the TalkBack menu, swipe down then right.
To use the reading controls, swipe left or right with three fingers. You can also swipe up or down with three fingers.
To cycle between reading controls, turn your secondary palm in, hold your pinch, move right or left and release.
To use the reading controls, swipe up then down.
Open TalkBack at the Play Store
Get help using TalkBack or report issues
Show instructions for turning off TalkBack
A message shows when TalkBack is turned on, to help you quickly turn it off
TalkBack on. Hold 2 fingers on screen for 5 seconds to turn off. To stop showing this message, tap with 3 fingers to open the TalkBack menu.
Showing banner indicating TalkBack is on. To turn off TalkBack, hold two fingers on the screen for five seconds. To remove this banner, go to TalkBack settings.

To keep using keyboard shortcuts, go to Settings and switch from the classic keymap to the default keymap.

What\'s different about the default keymap?

  • There are more navigation and web navigation actions.
  • Some of the modifier keys are different.
TalkBack is getting ready to remove the classic keymap. To keep using keyboard shortcuts, switch to the default keymap.
Classic keymap is no longer supported
You\'re now using the TalkBack default keymap. There is also a new enhanced keymap that has many new commands. Learn more in the Help Centre or switch to the new keymap in Settings.
The new TalkBack enhanced keymap adds many new commands. Learn more in the Help Centre or switch to the new keymap in Settings.
More information about your modifier key

For TalkBack\'s keyboard shortcuts to work correctly, update your modifier key from action to alt.\n\nIf you keep using the action modifier key, some shortcuts will take different actions for this version of Android.
TalkBack currently uses %1$s as the modifier key.
Modifier key has been changed to %1$s.
Navigate to previous category (based on reading controls)
Navigate to next category (based on reading controls)
Navigate to next window or system element
Navigate to previous window or system element
Search the screen for an item
Turn browse mode on or off
Navigate to end of current row
Navigate to beginning of current row
Navigate to end of current column
Navigate to beginning of current column
Press Backspace to clear the shortcut, press Enter to save or press Escape to cancel.
No text to copy; select some text first.
No text to paste; copy some texts first.
No text to cut; select some text first.
No text to delete; select some text first.
Are you sure that you would like to reset your shortcut keys for this keymap to their default values? Please note that this action cannot be undone.
Press key combination to set new shortcut. It must contain at least ALT or Control key.
Press key combination with %1$s modifier key to set new shortcut.
Can\'t use this shortcut. Try a different key combination.

To set a new shortcut, press %1$s + key. You can also press %1$s + control or shift + key. If you press %1$s + O or R, you need to press a third key shortly after. Press backspace to clear the shortcut, press Ctrl + backspace to reset to default. Press Enter to save, or press Escape to cancel.
If using %1$s + O or R, you need to press a third key
If using %1$s + O or R, you need to press a third key. The third key can\'t be Esc, backspace, Enter or a modifier key.
This key combination is used for \'%1$s\' action. Do you want to clear it and assign this shortcut to \'%2$s\'?
Settings to reduce the delay before TalkBack announcements
Speak deleted letters in a higher-pitched voice
Your device doesn\'t support braille keyboard

You can quickly adjust some of TalkBack's sound settings from any screen.

Accessibility volume: Hold 1 finger on the screen and press the volume up or volume down key.
TalkBack speech rate: Swipe up or down with 3 fingers. When you hear 'speech rate', swipe up or down with 1 finger.
Use voice search key to print node tree instead of searching
Touch and hold delay when typing
Shorter typing focus delay means that TalkBack announcements are made faster after touching the keyboard.\n\nSelect a delay

Controls how quickly TalkBack speaks after focus moves. A shorter delay makes TalkBack feel faster, but can make double- and triple-taps harder to perform.\n\n Delays below 0.3 seconds will change double-tap to activate to two-finger tap to activate.
Turn TalkBack off then on again to activate the setting.

Get detailed AI image descriptions when you select \'Describe image\' from the TalkBack menu.\n\nYou can also get automatic descriptions when you focus on an image or icon. Automatic descriptions work without an Internet connection.
Automatic descriptions for text in images
Turn on image descriptions in Settings
You can try again after turning on either automatic or detailed image descriptions
Hear longer descriptions when you select \'%1$s\' from the TalkBack menu
When using this feature, selected images will be sent to Google for processing and then deleted.\n\nGenerative AI is experimental, so it will make mistakes. By continuing, you agree to the %1$s.

You’ll hear a brief description when you focus on an image in supported apps.

Images are processed on your device and never sent to Google. 

Generative AI is experimental, so it will make mistakes. By continuing, you agree to Google's Terms of Service.
Download needed for automatic image descriptions
Images are processed on your device and never sent to Google.\n\nGenerative AI is experimental, so it will make mistakes. By continuing, you agree to the %1$s.
Downloading AI model. This may take a while. You\'ll be notified when it\'s complete.
Downloading still in progress. %1$d of %2$d MB downloaded.
AI model downloaded. TalkBack can now generate image descriptions.
For icons, images and text in images
For images and text in images
For icons and text in images

Detailed image descriptions are powered by generative AI. When using this feature, selected images will be sent to Google for processing and then deleted.

Generative AI is experimental, so it will make mistakes. You can turn off detailed image descriptions at any time in TalkBack settings.

By continuing, you agree to the %1$s.

When using this feature, a screenshot of your screen will be sent to Google for processing and then deleted. Processing takes several seconds.\n\nGenerative AI is experimental, so it will make mistakes. By continuing, you agree to %1$s.

You’ll hear a brief description when you focus on an image in supported apps.

Images are processed on your device and never sent to Google.

Generative AI is experimental, so it will make mistakes. By continuing, you agree to the %1$s.
TalkBack accessibility service has hidden the screen
To change this, %1$s to open the TalkBack menu, select \'%2$s\' then double-tap.
Unavailable when vibration watch is on
Focus on first item on screen
Focus on last item on screen
More menu options are available after you finish setup or unlock your device
TalkBack menu Swipe right to explore options in the TalkBack menu.\n\nTo go back to the tutorial, use two fingers to swipe left or right from the edge of your screen.
Move reading control down or forwards
Move reading control up or backwards
Play or pause media, start or end voice input
Speak time or don\'t speak time
Turn braille display on or off
Show or hide on-screen braille for connected braille displays
Turn sound feedback (earcons) on or off
Speak letters with phonetic pronunciation for focused item
Shows when actions exist for the current item, including links, copy, paste, select and more
Open links within a focused item
Go to the next or previous page. Shows when you are reading content with multiple pages.
Shows when you select an unlabelled item
Read by character, word, lines and more
Starting from the top, read all items on the screen
Starting from the focused item, read all items on the screen
Copy the last phrase that TalkBack said to the clipboard so that you can paste the text
Spell each letter of the last phrase that TalkBack said
Hear the last phrase that TalkBack said again
Enter a search term to find that text on the screen
Hide the display so that your screen is not visible to others in public places. After hiding your screen, return it to a normal state.
Change whether the time is spoken aloud when you turn on the screen
Go to all apps and Quick Settings
Jump between spelling and grammar suggestions
Read one character at a time
Read one word at a time
Read one line at a time
Read one paragraph at a time
Jump to other windows, like volume controls or the navigation bar
Exit out of a container, like a list or grid
Jump between interactive items like buttons or text fields
Jump between landmarks on web pages, like \'main\' or \'navigation\'
Jump between buttons on web pages
Jump between tick boxes on web pages
Jump between edit fields on web pages
Jump between focusable items on web pages
Jump between heading 1 on web pages
Jump between heading 2 on web pages
Jump between heading 3 on web pages
Jump between heading 4 on web pages
Jump between heading 5 on web pages
Jump between heading 6 on web pages
Jump between images and videos on web pages
Jump between list items on web pages
Jump between lists on web pages
Jump between tables on web pages
Jump between combo boxes on web pages, such as drop-down menus
Jump between links that you\'ve previously opened on web pages
Jump between links that you haven\'t opened on web pages
Jump between radio buttons on web pages
Jump to other screen search results
Jump between rows and columns in a table
Jump between rows in a table
Jump between columns in a table
Read one item at a time
Change whether keys, usage hints and more are read aloud
Change whether punctuation and symbols are read aloud
Change spoken language from available text-to-speech languages
Hear sound effects as you navigate, like a beep to confirm an action
Feel vibrations for actions like selecting an item. Does not affect vibrations coming from apps or the system.
Hide the display so that your screen isn\'t visible to others
Lower volume of other audio playing when TalkBack speaks
Increase or decrease typing focus delay
Summarise the screen with action buttons
Hear a summary of the content on the screen
Speak text and icons within a selected image
Shows when the cursor is on a typo
TalkBack preloads announcements to reduce delay when you type
Use Google Text-to-speech to use this feature
Secondary palm in, pinch and swipe up
Secondary palm in, pinch and swipe down
Triple-tap and hold with three fingers
Hold finger to select any key, then double-tap
Hold finger to select letter key, then lift. Double-tap for other keys.
Hold finger to select any key, then lift
Tap to review changes to accessibility gestures
Enter the text to be used as a label for this element\nInternal view name: %1$s
Choose whether you want to override or skip existing labels in case of conflict
Nothing to export. You have not added any labels
{count,plural, =0{No new labels}=1{# label updated}other{# labels updated}}
No applications found to import labels
No applications found to export labels.

This version of TalkBack includes changes to the default accessibility gestures. The actions associated with these gestures can be customised within TalkBack settings. When Explore by touch is enabled, you can use the following gestures to perform certain actions.\n\n%1$s
This turns off the display to improve privacy. Some content may still be shown, like messages for turning the display back on or menus for accessibility features like TalkBack.

This version of TalkBack includes changes to the default accessibility gestures.

The up-then-down gesture will allow you to move to the first item on the screen, and the down-then-up gesture will move to the last item.

The reading granularity can now be changed by focusing an item, then selecting a granularity from the talkback context menu.

You can change the actions performed by these gestures in TalkBack settings.
To pause and play media, you can now double-tap and hold with two fingers.\n\nOther gestures have also changed.
Open on phone to find out more

This version of TalkBack includes changes to the default accessibility gestures.\n\nThe \'up\' gesture now moves to the previous navigation setting, and the \'down\' gesture now moves to the next navigation setting.
These settings are removed from TalkBack, due to reliability issues:\n• Tap on side of device gestures\n• Shake to start continuous reading
Are you sure that you want to turn off Explore by touch?\n\nYour device will no longer respond to shortcut gestures or speak items when you touch the screen.
Are you sure that you want to turn on node tree debugging?\n\nNode tree debugging exposes the content of your screen to your device\'s logs, which may be read by other applications.
Reassign the triple-tap with 4 fingers gesture?

Confirming that this will assign the triple-tap with 4 fingers gesture to print the node tree.

If you cancel, node tree debugging will be enabled but a gesture will not be assigned and you will need to set this in the gesture settings.

On disabling node tree debugging, the gesture's previous action, if any, will not be restored.
Are you sure that you want to turn on performance statistics?\n\nPerformance statistics may consume more memory and power.
Verbose logging can aid in debugging but may cause personal information to appear in the system log buffer.\n\n Do you want to enable verbose logging?

Diagnosis mode helps debugging. Diagnosis mode overrides gesture tap with four fingers to capture screen contents to system logs. Screen contents may include personal information. \n\n Do you want to enable diagnosis mode?
This helps TalkBack reduce announcements when you\'re on a call
No previous result on this page
No next result on this page
Press arrow buttons to change value, or press select to finish.
Shorter focus delay means that TalkBack announcements are made faster after touching the screen
For the shortest delay when typing, choose \'Hold finger to select any key, then lift\'
You can also use split tap to type at any time. To do this, hold one finger to select any key, and use another finger to tap on screen.
{matches,plural, =0{No matches on this screen}=1{# match}other{# matches}}

After selecting \'Read from top\' or \'Read from focused item\', you can still navigate to the previous or next item while the text is being read. This lets you go back or skip ahead with ease.\n\nTo adjust the volume while TalkBack speaks, press the volume up or volume down key.
Use voice commands to control TalkBack

Here are some things that you can say:

• Copy
• Read from focused item
• Find [save]
• Speak [faster]
• TalkBack settings

Say '%1$s' for a list of all voice commands
Skip TalkBack gestures to use system gestures
When you hear a chime, you can use a system gesture that isn\'t supported by TalkBack. This is known as a pass-through gesture.
Update Gboard to use voice input
2-finger double-tap can now turn on voice input whenever Gboard is active. To use this gesture, update Gboard to the latest version.
Show Gboard to use voice dictation
%1$s to select a setting. Then %2$s to adjust the setting.
Secondary palm in, long press to toggle reading control mode.
To use this, go to TalkBack settings and add a gesture for \'%1$s\'
To use this, go to TalkBack settings and add a gesture for spell check
No keyword yet. To start a new search, tap and hold with 3 fingers or open screen search from the TalkBack menu.
No keyword yet. To start a new search, swipe left then down or open screen search from the TalkBack menu.
Braille keyboard isn\'t turned on. Go to TalkBack settings, set up Braille keyboard, then try again.

TalkBack is Google's screen reader. It reads aloud the text on the screen and gives you spoken feedback, so that you can use your device without looking at the screen.

With TalkBack on, a selected item on the screen is spoken aloud, sometimes with additional information or context added.

Press Select to continue. The Select button is located at the centre of the directional pad, which is the large, circular button at the top of your remote.

Move around the screen using the circle-shaped directional button at the top of your remote. To select, press the centre of the directional button at the top of your remote.

In the second row on the left, you have the back button. On the right you have the voice Assistant button.

In the third row on the left, you have the Home button. On the right is the mute button.

In the fourth row on the left, you have the YouTube shortcut. On the right is the Netflix shortcut.

In the bottom row there are two small buttons. The left button is the power button. The right will open your TV’s input menu.

The volume buttons are located at the right side of the remote.

Press Select to continue.

To access the TalkBack shortcut, press the down and back buttons at the same time and hold them down for three seconds. This will either turn on or turn off TalkBack.

You can also access this shortcut within your TV settings.

Press Select to continue.
You can come back to the tutorial at any time in TalkBack settings.
You can come back to review new features at any time in TalkBack settings.
To pause or play media in a video or music app, double-tap with two fingers. Try it now.
Other apps may use this gesture for things like answering or ending a phone call. This gesture may not be supported by all apps.
Media paused or playing. Usually, this will pause or play the current video or music. Swipe right to continue.
To stop TalkBack from talking temporarily, tap with two fingers.
TalkBack speech stopped. Usually, this will stop the current phrase that TalkBack is saying. Swipe right to continue.
To copy text, double-tap with three fingers.
Copied text. Usually, this will copy the text that you have selected. Swipe right to continue.
To paste text, triple-tap with three fingers.
Pasted text. Usually, this will paste the text that you have copied. Swipe right to continue.
To cut text, double-tap and hold with three fingers.
Cut text. Usually, this will cut the text that you have selected. Swipe right to continue.
To start or end text selection mode, double-tap and hold with two fingers.
Selection mode on or off. Usually, this will start or stop text selection mode when you are inside an input field. Swipe right to continue.
To use the reading controls, swipe left or right with three fingers. You can also swipe up or down with three fingers.
Note: You have customised some of your gestures, so this information may not be accurate. To review your gestures, go to TalkBack settings.
New gestures have been added to make TalkBack faster and easier to use.\n\nOn this screen, you can try the new gestures. You can review this information in TalkBack settings at any time.
Tap to learn how to use TalkBack faster with new gestures.
Node tree has been printed to logs.
Here are gestures performed recently. Please report which gesture is wrong and what is expected.

You can now connect to braille displays via HID, a way to connect to Bluetooth devices. Make sure that you have the latest firmware drivers from your manufacturer, then turn on Bluetooth on your braille display. Go to TalkBack settings > Braille display and turn on \'Use braille display\'. Find your device in the devices list and connect to it.

You can now connect to braille displays via HID, a way to connect to Bluetooth devices. Make sure that you have the latest firmware drivers from your manufacturer, then turn on Bluetooth on your braille display. Go to TalkBack settings then Braille display and turn on \'Use braille display\'. Find your device in the devices list and connect to it.
Learn how to use your braille display with TalkBack.

This TalkBack update introduces support for Describe screen in more languages and other quality improvements that make TalkBack easier to use.

You'll learn more about these changes in the next few screens.

You can review this information later in TalkBack settings > New features in TalkBack.

This TalkBack update introduces support for Describe screen in more languages and other quality improvements that make TalkBack easier to use.

You'll learn more about these changes in the next few screens.

You can review this information later in TalkBack settings, then New features in TalkBack.
New languages for Describe screen and Ask Gemini
Describe screen and Ask Gemini are now available in additional languages. Learn more in the Help Centre.

While using Gboard, you can now use a two-finger double-tap to start or stop voice typing.

Make sure that you have the latest version of Gboard from Google Play.
When Gboard is on screen, you can now use a two-finger double-tap to start voice dictation, then a two-finger double-tap to stop.

TalkBack now has an \'Enhanced keymap\' for its keyboard, with a variety of new commands and shortcuts. This enhancement, which includes \'browse mode\' for more efficient web navigation, streamlines navigation and interaction for TalkBack users who rely on physical keyboards. You can switch to \'Enhanced keymap\' in TalkBack settings > Advanced settings > Keyboard shortcuts.

TalkBack now has an \'Enhanced keymap\' for its keyboard, with a variety of new commands and shortcuts. This enhancement, which includes \'browse mode\' for more efficient web navigation, streamlines navigation and interaction for TalkBack users who rely on physical keyboards. You can switch to \'Enhanced keymap\' in TalkBack settings, then Advanced settings, then Keyboard shortcuts.
You can now reduce TalkBack announcement delays for touch exploration and typing. To view these settings, go to TalkBack settings > Advanced settings > Reduce delay.
You can now reduce TalkBack announcement delays for touch exploration and typing. To view these settings, go to TalkBack settings, then Advanced settings, then Reduce delay.
It\'s now possible to customise gestures for the virtual on-screen braille keyboard. To customise, go to TalkBack settings > Braille keyboard > Gestures.
It\'s now possible to customise gestures for the virtual on-screen braille keyboard. To customise, go to TalkBack settings, then Braille keyboard, then Gestures.
An on-screen message now helps you quickly turn off TalkBack if it\'s accidentally turned on. You can turn this off in TalkBack settings > Show instructions for turning off TalkBack.
An on-screen message now helps you quickly turn off TalkBack if it\'s accidentally turned on. You can turn this off in TalkBack settings, then Show instructions for turning off TalkBack.
Classic keymap has been switched to default keymap, which includes more shortcuts.
TalkBack can now announce changes in text formatting, like bold, italics and text colour. To get started:
Focus on the item with formatting, open the TalkBack menu and select \'Describe text formatting\', or
Add a reading control for text formatting that will turn on or off the announcement of formatting changes.
When you\'re ready to try it, here\'s an example of formatted text:
If you want to change how formatted text is announced, you can go to %1$s.
TalkBack settings > Verbosity > Text formatting options
TalkBack settings, then Verbosity, then Text formatting options

This text is in bold, a confident shout,
While this is underlined, a whisper, no doubt.

Roses are red, a classic, it\'s true.
Violets are blue, a lovely soft hue.

Some are small, like secrets barely there,
And some are huge, demanding all the air.

Colours can splash, a vibrant display,
From fiery red to verdant green\'s gentle sway.

This TalkBack update introduces a new 'Enhanced keymap' for physical keyboards, support for text formatting announcements and a redesigned settings layout, along with other improvements to make TalkBack easier to use.

You will learn more about these changes in the next few screens.

You can review this information later in TalkBack settings > New features in TalkBack.

This TalkBack update introduces a new 'Enhanced keymap' for physical keyboards, support for text formatting announcements and a redesigned settings layout, along with other improvements to make TalkBack easier to use.

You will learn more about these changes in the next few screens.

You can review this information later in TalkBack settings, then New features in TalkBack.
Settings groups have been updated to make features easier to find. New settings categories are also available, such as keyboard settings.

You can now use split tap everywhere to activate items, even outside of the keyboard. To use it, explore the screen with one finger, then without lifting the first finger, tap on the screen with another finger to activate the currently focused item.

TalkBack now has an \'Enhanced keymap\' for physical keyboards, featuring a variety of new commands and shortcuts. This enhancement includes a \'browse mode\' for more efficient web navigation, streamlining navigation and interaction for TalkBack users who rely on physical keyboards. You can switch to the \'Enhanced keymap\' in %1$s.\n\nIf you use a braille display, you can also use new browse mode shortcuts to traverse through content directly from your braille display. Some braille display shortcuts were changed to accommodate this change. You can see a list of all braille display shortcuts in %2$s.

TalkBack smart browse mode turns off browse mode on an edit field to help you type with a physical keyboard when you browse the web. It then turns it back on when you jump to a non-edit field on the web so that you can continue browsing efficiently. You can turn \'Smart browse mode\' on or off in %1$s.

TalkBack now includes a keyboard tutorial designed to help you learn the keyboard shortcuts and commands for the Enhanced keymap when using a physical keyboard. To access the tutorial, navigate to: %1$s.
TalkBack reads content on your screen aloud and changes how navigation works on your device. To turn it off, tap \'Turn off TalkBack\' twice.
TalkBack reads content on your screen aloud and changes how navigation works on your device. To turn it off, select \'Turn off TalkBack\' or tap it 2 times.

TalkBack provides spoken feedback so that you can use your device without looking at the screen. TalkBack can be helpful for people who have difficulty seeing the screen. 

If you want to quickly turn TalkBack on or off using your volume keys, you can set up a shortcut in TalkBack settings > TalkBack shortcut.

When TalkBack is on, the focused item on the screen is spoken out loud.

Swiping right will move to the next item, and swiping left will move to the previous item.

Swipe right now to find the next button.

TalkBack provides spoken feedback so that you can use your device without looking at the screen. TalkBack can be helpful for people who have difficulty seeing the screen. 

If you want to quickly turn TalkBack on or off using your volume keys, you can set up a shortcut in TalkBack settings then TalkBack shortcut.

When TalkBack is on, the focused item on the screen is spoken out loud.

Swiping right will move to the next item, and swiping left will move to the previous item.

Swipe right now to find the next button.
Swipe right now to find the next button
You can also focus on an item by dragging your finger across the screen.\n\nTry this now by touching the screen and dragging towards the bottom left corner until you find the next button.
Scroll by swiping up or down with two fingers
You can practice on this screen until you find the \'Next\' button.
Try scrolling by swiping up or down with two fingers
Great work! You\'ve scrolled to the bottom of the screen.\n\nSwipe right to find the next button.
Add one finger to system gestures

This means when using TalkBack, you can go to the home screen by swiping up from the bottom with two fingers instead of one.\n\nTrying these gestures now will close the tutorial. You may want to wait until you have completed the tutorial.
To go to your home screen, swipe up from the bottom of your screen with two fingers.
To go to your recent apps, swipe up from the bottom of your screen with two fingers and hold. Then release. This takes you to the overview screen.
To go back, start at the left or right edge and swipe towards the middle of your screen with two fingers.
To open notifications, swipe down from the top of the screen with two fingers.
To open notifications, swipe down from the top of the screen with two fingers. Then you can close notifications by swiping back up with two fingers.\n\nTry it now.
To open your accessibility shortcut, swipe up from the bottom of the screen with three fingers.
The volume for TalkBack speech is called the accessibility volume. It\'s adjusted separately from the media volume.\n\nTo adjust the accessibility volume:
Hold one finger on the screen
While holding, press the volume up or volume down key on the side of your device
You\'ll hear the new accessibility volume after a few seconds.\n\nTry adjusting the accessibility volume now.
The TalkBack menu includes settings and controls that you may need often.\n\nOpen the TalkBack menu now by tapping the screen with three fingers.
The TalkBack menu includes settings and controls that you may often need.\n\nOpen the TalkBack menu now by swiping down then right.

These shortcuts can help you quickly change settings, like switching your navigation from characters to words or changing TalkBack's speech rate.

To quickly change your reading controls, swipe left with three fingers or swipe right with three fingers.

You can customise the TalkBack menu and your reading controls later in TalkBack settings.

These shortcuts can help you quickly change settings, like switching your navigation from characters to words or changing TalkBack's speech rate.

To quickly change your reading controls, swipe down and then back up.

You can customise the TalkBack menu and your reading controls later in TalkBack settings.
You are ready to use TalkBack.\n\nKeep learning about other topics now, or review this info at any time in TalkBack settings.
Swipe right to select a topic. For the best experience, turn on TalkBack before getting started.
Move between items, scroll, use menus and more
Type, select, edit, copy and more
Read by word or character, and jump between links, controls or headings
Look for specific items on screen
Learn how to make calls, send messages and more
Try different gestures to learn what they do
Learn more about TalkBack and send feedback
Choose how you would like to interact with TalkBack

When you're in a text box, a keyboard will open up at the bottom of your screen.

To dismiss the keyboard, double-tap the back button in the bottom corner of your screen.

Try activating and dismissing the text box below.
When you\'re in a text box, a keyboard will open up at the bottom of your screen.
Move your finger across the keyboard. When you hear the letter that you want to type, lift your finger.\n\nTry activating the text box below and type your name.
You can also type with the braille keyboard. Find it in TalkBack settings.
There are other ways to type in TalkBack. For example, you can use double-tap to enter a letter.
There are other ways to type in TalkBack. For example, you can use double-tap to enter a letter. You can change this later in TalkBack settings > On-screen keyboard > Typing preference.
There are other ways to type in TalkBack. For example, you can use double-tap to enter a letter. You can change this later in TalkBack settings, then On-screen keyboard, then Typing preference.
When you have activated a text box, you can move the cursor between letters by swiping up or down.\n\nTry it in the text box below.

In a text box, move the cursor to the beginning of the text that you want to select.

Double-tap and hold with two fingers to start selection mode. Then move the cursor to the end of the text. Double-tap and hold with two fingers again to end selection mode.

Try to select the word 'Welcome' in the text box below.

In a text box, move the cursor to the beginning of the text that you want to select.

Swipe down and then right to open the TalkBack menu. Double-tap actions and then start selection mode. Move the cursor to the end of the text.

Open the TalkBack menu again, then double-tap actions to end selection mode.

Try to select the word 'Welcome' in the text box below.
After selecting text, copy it by double-tapping with three fingers.\n\nTry to copy a word in the text box below.

To cut text, double-tap and hold with three fingers.

To paste text, triple-tap with three fingers.

Try to paste a word in the text box below.
After selecting text, you can copy it in the TalkBack menu. Swipe down and then right to open the TalkBack menu. Double-tap actions and then copy.\n\nTry to copy a word in the text box below.
To cut or paste text, open the TalkBack menu and double-tap actions.\n\nTry to paste a word in the text box below.

You can use proofreading to check the spelling or grammar within a text box.\n\nTo try it, focus on the text box below, which contains spelling errors. Then, swipe right with three fingers until you find \'Proofread\'.

You can use proofreading to check the spelling or grammar within a text box.\n\nTo try it, focus on the text box below, which contains spelling errors. After focusing, swipe down and then up until you find \'Proofread\'.

You can use proofreading to check the spelling or grammar within a text box.\n\nTo try it, focus on the text box below and type a misspelled word. Then, swipe right with three fingers until you find \'Proofread\'.

You can use proofreading to check the spelling or grammar within a text box.\n\nTo try it, focus on the text box below and type a misspelled word. After focusing, swipe down and then up until you find \'Proofread\'.
Read by character, word or paragraph

Sometimes, you might want to hear text read out loud one character at a time.

To do this, swipe right with three fingers until you hear '%1$s'. Next, swipe up to move the navigation focus backwards by character. Swipe down to move the navigation focus forwards by character.

Try it now. When you swipe right with three fingers, you can also choose to read by words or paragraphs.

Sometimes, you might want to hear text read out loud one character at a time.

To do this, swipe down and then up until you hear '%1$s'. Next, swipe up to move the navigation focus backward by character. Swipe down to move the navigation focus forward by character.

Try it now. When you swipe down and then up, you can also choose to read by words or paragraphs.

Controls are items that you can interact with, such as buttons, tick boxes and text boxes.

On some pages, you may want to jump between controls to find an action quickly, like the next button.

To do this, swipe right with three fingers until you hear '%1$s'. Next, swipe up to go to the previous control on a page. Swipe down to go to the next control on a page.

Try it now. Switch to controls and find the next button.

Controls are items that you can interact with, like buttons, tick boxes and text boxes.

On some pages, you may want to jump between controls to quickly find an action, like the next button.

To do this, swipe down and then up until you hear '%1$s'. Next, swipe up to go to the previous control on a page. Swipe down to go to the next control on a page.

Try it now. Switch to controls and find the next button.

You can also jump between links on a page.

To try it now, swipe right with 3 fingers until you hear '%1$s'. Next, swipe down to find the link named 'Link 5'. While in the tutorial, double-tapping this link will not do anything.

Swipe right to find the next button and go to the next page.

You can also jump between links on a page.

To try it now, swipe down and then up until you hear '%1$s'. Next, swipe down and find the link named 'Link 5'. While in the tutorial, double-tapping this link will not do anything.

Swipe right to find the next button and go to the next page.
You can also jump between headings on a page.\n\nTo try it now, swipe right with three fingers until you hear \'%1$s\'. Then, swipe down to find the \'navigation\' heading.
You can also jump between headings on a page.\n\nTo try it now, swipe down and then up until you hear \'%1$s\'. Then, swipe down to find the \'navigation\' heading.
Swipe right to find the next button.
You’ve exited the list of containers. You can add containers to your reading controls at any time for easier access.\n\nSwipe right to find the finish button.

To exit out of a container, like a list or grid, swipe left or right with four fingers. This is useful when you want to move out of a long list of items.\n\nTo try it now, swipe right to enter the list. Then, swipe right with four fingers to go to the next container.

Exiting out of a container, like a list or grid, is useful when you want to move out of a long list of items.\n\nTo try it now, swipe down and then up until you hear \'%1$s\'. Then, swipe down to go to the next container.
To go to the next container, swipe right with four fingers
To exit this container, swipe right with four fingers
To go to the next container, swipe down
To exit this container, swipe down

You can use your voice to control TalkBack quickly.

For example, say a command to change navigation, jump to the next heading, copy text and more.

To try it now, swipe right and then up.

The screen search feature allows you to look for specific content on the screen.

To try it, open the TalkBack menu by tapping with 3 fingers and find 'screen search' in the menu. Type in a keyword, like 'next', to find items with that text on the screen.

Try searching for 'next' now to find the next button.

You can quickly move between different search results from screen search after you've selected a result.

To do this, use reading controls by swiping left or right with 3 fingers and find the item called screen search.

Let's try it now. Below is a list of 3 buttons, all of them containing the text 'action'. Search for 'action' using screen search, then navigate by screen search to move between them.
You can call a family member, friend or business with the phone app on your home screen.\n\nWhen you receive a call, you can double-tap with two fingers to answer it.
You can also ask Google Assistant to call a number or the name of someone in your contacts
To send a message, go to your home screen and find a messaging app
You can also ask Google Assistant to send a message to a contact or number
Reading mode makes long content easier to navigate, listen to and read. You can change the font size and contrast, have the text read aloud and more.

After installing reading mode:

1. Go to Settings > Accessibility > Reading mode
2. Turn on the shortcut
3. Go to an app or a website, and use the shortcut to open reading mode

After installing Reading mode:

1. Go to Settings then Accessibility then Reading mode
2. Turn on the shortcut
3. Go to an app or a website, and use the shortcut to open Reading mode
Exploring items and text around you
Lookout uses your camera to get details about objects, images and text around you. You can use it to read signs, sort post and more.
Lookout has several modes, depending on the task that you\'re trying to do. For example, to scan a page of text, select Document mode.
TalkBack will announce when you get a new notification.
First, swipe down with 4 fingers to access the notification.
Then, swipe left or right to navigate within the notification.
You can check which apps have notifications turned on in Settings > Notifications.
You can check which apps have notifications turned on in Settings, then Notifications.
To check your notification history, swipe down with two fingers from the top of your screen
When you\'re focused on an image, open the TalkBack menu and select \'Describe image\'.\n\nThere\'s one photo below. Try getting a description for it.
You can change image description settings at any time in TalkBack settings > Image and icon descriptions.
You can change image description settings at any time in TalkBack settings, then Image and icon descriptions.
Sample image; tap with 3 fingers to open the TalkBack menu and select \'Describe image\' to hear a description.

Try any TalkBack gesture on this screen to hear what that gesture does.

Tap the screen with two fingers. Keep practising by tapping once, twice or three times with two, three or four fingers.

You can also double-tap and hold with two, three or four fingers.

Try any TalkBack gesture on this screen to hear what that gesture does.\n\nTry any gesture where two swipes form a right angle. For example, swipe down and then right, without lifting your finger in between.
Press a physical keyboard key or key combination to hear what it does. Press Ctrl + W, or Escape to exit.\n\nYou can also practise TalkBack gestures here.
This tutorial works best with TalkBack on. You can make this change in Settings.\n\nIf you continue with TalkBack off, some of the tutorials may not work as intended.
To practice gestures, first turn on TalkBack
To try getting image descriptions, first turn on TalkBack

When TalkBack is on, the focused item on the screen is spoken out loud.

Swiping right moves to the next item. Swiping left moves to the previous item.

Swipe right now until you find the Next button.
Swipe up or down with %1$d fingers.
When you want to go back, use %1$d fingers to swipe right from the edge of the screen.
To open settings and controls that you may need often, swipe down and then right
To type with keyboard, move your finger across the keyboard. When you hear the letter that you want to type, lift your finger.
To hide the keyboard and check your input, swipe down from the top of your screen with 2 fingers.
To return to the keyboard, swipe up from the bottom of your screen with 2 fingers.
To close the keyboard and cancel typing, swipe right from the left edge of your screen with 2 fingers.
You\'re ready to start using TalkBack
You\'ll notice some bug fixes and changes to how the focus moves
Please check if the phone is connected.
Describe this image. Do not use any markup in the response.

This is a conversation between a user and an AI assistant about the image. Please continue the conversation based on the history provided below, responding to the final USER message. Follow these instructions: **Instructions:** * Start your response with a one-sentence answer to the question * After that, you can elaborate with more detail if it seems helpful * Only give opinions if the user\'s intent is to get an opinion. Otherwise, stick to facts * If the user asks about the location of something on the screen, describe its position relative to the whole screen (e.g. \'in the tab bar near the top on the right side\') not relative to other visible elements on the screen. Think carefully to avoid mixing up left and right * If the user asks you to interact with the screen (e.g. tap, scroll, tap, search), you must respond \'Sorry, I can only answer questions about the screen at this time\' * If the user asks about information that is not visible in the screenshot, clarify that the information is not shown on the screen and then answer the question based on your general knowledge * Do not use any markdown in your response. **Conversation history:**

This is a conversation between a user and an AI assistant about the screen. Please continue the conversation based on the history provided below, responding to the final USER message. Follow these instructions: **Instructions:** * Start your response with a one-sentence answer to the question * After that, you can elaborate with more detail if it seems helpful * Only give opinions if the user\'s intent is to get an opinion. Otherwise, stick to facts * If the user asks about the location of something on the screen, describe its position relative to the whole screen (e.g. \'in the tab bar near the top on the right side\') not relative to other visible elements on the screen. Think carefully to avoid mixing up left and right * If the user asks you to interact with the screen (e.g. tap, scroll, tap, search), you must respond \'Sorry, I can only answer questions about the screen at this time\' * If the user asks about information that is not visible in the screenshot, clarify that the information is not shown on the screen and then answer the question based on your general knowledge * Do not use any markdown in your response. **Conversation history:**

Your job is to help someone who cannot see their phone to understand what is on their phone screen. Describe this screenshot in a few sentences.
Instructions Start your response with \'The screen shows\' The beginning of the response should describe the most important, salient content on the screen. Here are some examples of what the most important, salient content to describe might be * In a chat app: The sender and text of the most recent message * In a social media app: The first post, including who posted it, the text and a description of the image * In a news app: The article title and a description of the primary image * In a shopping app: A description of the product shown and the price * In a calendar app: The next event and the number of other events that are shown afterwards * In a list of messages: The name and preview for the first message and then only the names for the next few in the list, and how many messages are unread
After that, the response can describe the spatial location and purpose of navigation bars and prominent buttons. Do not mention the contents of the status bar at the top (e.g. time, network status, battery). Do not use any markdown in your response.
Here are the elements that I found in the app:
Image descriptions aren\'t available when the screen is hidden. Show your screen and try again.
Download needed for automatic icon descriptions
%1$d MB using mobile data. Data charges may apply.
You\'ll hear a brief description when you focus on an icon, like a button in an app.\n\nImages are processed on your device and never sent to Google.
Download needed for automatic image descriptions
You\'ll hear a brief description when you focus on an image in supported apps
Download needed for automatic image and icon descriptions
You\'ll hear a brief description when you focus on an image or icon in supported apps
TalkBack can still describe text in images
Downloading automatic image and icon descriptions
Automatic image and icon descriptions downloaded
Can\'t download. Please try again later.
You\'ll now hear automatic image and icon descriptions with TalkBack
Storage is full. To download, free up %1$d MB in Settings > Storage.
Can\'t download. Check your connection and try again.
Hear descriptions when you focus on an icon
Hear descriptions when you focus on an image. Some apps may not support this.
Hear descriptions when you focus on an image that contains text
You can still get descriptions for a selected image from the TalkBack menu
Delete automatic image and icon descriptions?
To get descriptions again later, you can download this feature from TalkBack settings
This deletes automatic descriptions for both icons and images.\n\nTo get descriptions again later, you can download these features from TalkBack settings.
Automatic image and icon descriptions deleted
Turn on to enable screen understanding with Gemini
To hear image descriptions, get the latest version of %1$s from Google Play.\n\nOnce downloading is complete, come back to TalkBack settings and turn on automatic image descriptions again.
No Internet connection. Check your Wi-Fi or mobile network and try again.
%1$s. Can\'t get a more detailed image description.
%1$s. Connect to the Internet for a more detailed image description.
I\'m sorry, I can\'t find that part of the screen any more.
Here are the major actions that you can take from this app screen:
Automatically switch to table navigation reading control when entering a table
For changes like bold, italic, underline and more
Turn speak text formatting on or off
For changes like bold and italic
Change whether formatting, like bold and italic, are read aloud
When speech is on, you\'ll hear announcements like \'start bold, headline, end bold\'
Automatically turns browse mode on or off based on the item that you\'re on. This syncs input focus with TalkBack focus.
To hear notifications when your screen is off, turn on \'Speak notifications when screen is off\' in TalkBack verbosity settings
